translations.translation:
Lai Min (Jingda)
來敏 (敬達)
Lived ADca.163–ca.260
aSanguozhiBiographyRecords of the Three Kingdoms [三國志]
byZL181
onDecember 19th, 2023
AbstractEntity::setUpdateForRefresh: 'update_for_refresh' value 'NULL' changed to 'true' via 'loadFlags::$this->update_for_refresh'

AbstractEntity::updateTimestamp: Timestamp 'rendered_at' changed to '1760029993' from 'NULL' via 'AbstractEntity::loadEntityCache::data[db][*]'

Lai Min, styledJingda, was fromXinye[新野],Yiyang[義陽].
He was a descendant of Lai Xi [來歙]. His father, [Lai] Yan [來豔], served asExcellency of WorksunderHan.A
TheBook of Later HanbyHua Jiaostates: [Lai] Yan [來豔] enjoyed learning and deferred to scholars. He opened residences to support displaced people. From a young age, he served in many prestigious posts. During the time of theKeenEmperor, he attained the position ofExcellency of Works.
There was great turmoil towards the end ofHan, so[Lai] Minfollowed his older sister to rush toJing province[荊州]. The husband of his older sister,Huang Wan, was the fraternal nephew of the paternal grandmother ofLiu Zhang. Therefore,[Liu] Zhangsent [people] to receive the wife of[Huang] Wan.[Lai] Minthus accompanied his older sister to Shu [蜀] and would often serve as a guest to[Liu] Zhang. He cursorily read books and records, being adept at theCommentary of Zuoon theChronicles. He was especially proficient in teaching the philology of[Three Chapters by] Cangand[Nearing the] Proper, and he was good at correcting written characters.
When theFormer LordstabilizedYi province[益州],[Lai] Minwas made provisional Colonel who Directs Education, and when theHeir Apparentwas installed, he served as Prefect of the Household.
After theLatter Lordascended the throne steps, he served asHousehold General of the Rapid Tigers, and when theAssisting Chancellor,[Zhuge] Liang, was stationed inHanzhong, he was invited to serve asArmy LibationerandGeneral who Assists the Army, but because he was implicated in matters, he was dismissed from hisduties.B
TheCollection of[Zhuge] Liangcontains his order, which states:
“GeneralLai Minreplied toShangguan Xianwith, ‘What deeds and virtue do you have, newcomer, that you stolen my prestige and salary? It must be that many people resent me! Why else would this happen?’[Lai] Minis deluded in his old age, which resulted in such resentful words. Of old, whenChengdu[成都] was previously stabilized, debaters believedLai Minwould confuse the masses, but thedeparted emperortolerated him, though without giving him respect or appointment, because the area was recently stabilized. Later,LiuZichuselected him to be the Prefect of the Household under theHeir Apparent, and though thedeparted emperorwas displeased, he was unwilling to oppose it. When theLatter Lordascended the throne, I was ignorant in discerning people so much so that I also nominated him to be a General and Libationer, going against the shrewd views of debaters and violating what thedeparted emperordistanced himself from. I presumed that it was possible to decrease his vulgarity through sincerity and stringency, and to guide him with righteousness. As that is impossible, I petition for him to be dismissed from his duties so that he may think about his faults within closed gates.”
After[Zhuge] Liangdied, he returned toChengdu[成都] to serve asGrand Chamberlain, and even though he was removed once more, he was later promoted to beBrilliantly Blessed Grandee. He was implicated of faults once more and was dismissed.
All of his demotions were all because of his lack of restraint in his speech and his defiance to standards in his actions. At the time,Meng Guangwas also being imprudent in a pivotal post and his opinions offended those of his time, yet he was still better than[Lai] Min. Both of them were respected by the world, being elderly and veteran scholars who were learned. As[Lai] Minwas of a famous family of the Jing-Chu region [荊楚] and a veteran minister of the Eastern Palace [東宮], he was particularly awarded preferential treatment. Therefore, whenever he was expelled, he would be reinstated. Later,[Lai] Minwas made General who Maintains Caution with the purpose of using that title to emphasize that he should be aware and cautious of himself.
He was ninety-seven when he died during theera. His son,[Lai] Zhong, also had extensive knowledge on the studies of the classics, having the style of[Lai] Min. He and the likes ofSecretariat MinisterXiang Chongall assisted theGrand General,Jiang Wei.[Jiang] Weiliked them and had them serve asMilitary Adjutants.
